714: **Prime is the next real family extraction.**  
715: The 2021 paper says all local positive spaces relevant for one-loop MHV are characterized by sign-flip conditions; in the maximal sign-flip case they are **finite one-loop octagons**. It then says particular combinations of these sign-flip spaces glue into local pentagon geometries that do **not** triangulate the original amplituhedron but rather its twin **Amplituhedron-Prime**, which has the **same boundary structure** and therefore the same logarithmic form, while differing in the **bulk** as a geometric space. That is a genuinely new family-level signal, not just a reformulation. [Springer+2Springer+2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP01%282021%29035)
716: 
717: That means the next exact substrate question is no longer “does sign-flip survive another presentation?” It is sharper:
718: 
719: $$\text{which substrate constraints are boundary-determined, and which are bulk-sensitive?}$$
720: 
721: Prime is the first paper in your set that directly attacks that row, because it supplies two geometries with the **same boundary/log form data** but different bulk geometry. If a candidate substrate constraint survives from amplituhedron to Prime, it is plausibly boundary-determined. If it fails, it is bulk-sensitive. None of associahedron/cosmohedron/amplituhedron-$m=1$/surfaceology currently gives you that discriminator. [Springer+1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP01%282021%29035)

2553: **1) Topological-order / anyonic / TQFT-like substrates — closest current candidate, but still below Class C.**  
2554: What is native here is real: information can be stored in multi-anyon topological degeneracy, processed by braiding, and read out by fusion/measurement; boundary-bulk duality and anyon condensation give genuine access to bulk topological data through boundary excitations, including experimental protocols for extracting $R$ and $F$ data from boundary behavior. That makes this class the strongest current hit on intrinsic boundary structure and bulk/nonlocal distinction. But it still does **not** give you the positive-geometry-style object you need: no sourced unique recursive readout analogous to canonical-form residue recursion, and no clear native law that makes semantics boundary-first in the $x\to \partial x\to \Omega(x)$ sense rather than “braid/fuse then measure.” My screening call is: **G1 pass, G2 partial/pass, G3 fail/unclear, G4 partial, G5 unclear, G6 partial. Class B+, closest to C.** [PMC+3Microsoft+3ScienceDirect+3](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/?p=425721&utm_source=chatgpt.com)
2555: 
2556: **2) Open compositional rewriting substrates (structured cospans / open Petri / open graphs) — strong compositional control, but boundary is designated, not intrinsic.**  
2557: Open Petri nets explicitly designate certain places as inputs and outputs via a cospan of sets, and their semantics is then built compositionally by gluing outputs to inputs; structured cospans generalize the same idea to systems with chosen interfaces. This makes them very good fits for locality and compositional semantics, but it is exactly the wrong direction for the wedge: the boundary is **opened by the modeler** rather than arising as an intrinsic stratification of the object itself. My screening call is: **G1 pass, G2 fail, G3 fail, G4 fail, G5 fail, G6 pass. Class B.** [Cambridge University Press & Assessment+2ResearchGate+2](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/mathematical-structures-in-computer-science/article/open-petri-nets/4D1BC7F05BE8CB0C83C65F7CDF70AF2F?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
2558: 
2559: **3) Constraint-native optimization substrates (SAT/SMT, QUBO/Ising, annealers) — native admissibility, wrong semantics.**  
2560: SMT solvers natively check satisfiability of formulas and can produce models; D-Wave’s annealers natively minimize Ising/QUBO energy landscapes defined by qubit biases and couplers. That means legality/objective structure is native here. But there is no intrinsic boundary stratification, no canonical recursively boundary-determined semantics, and no Prime-style notion where the same canonical boundary/readout semantics leaves distinct bulk objects alive as a first-class feature. My screening call is: **G1 pass, G2 fail, G3 fail, G4 fail, G5 fail/unclear, G6 weak/irrelevant. Class A/B.** [Microsoft GitHub+2docs.dwavequantum.com+2](https://microsoft.github.io/z3guide/docs/optimization/intro/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
2561: 
2562: **4) Wave-based analog / metamaterial boundary-value substrates — tempting because of boundary conditions, but still boundary-imposed, not intrinsic.**  
2563: Wave-based analog computing with metamaterials and metasurfaces is real and can solve ODE/PDE problems physically, including boundary-value problems. That is the first serious class that superficially resembles “boundary-first” computation. But the resemblance is weaker than it looks: the boundary conditions are imposed externally as the posed problem, not extracted as an intrinsic boundary stratification of the substrate’s legal object; the readout is the solved field/configuration, not a unique canonical semantics forced recursively by codim-1 boundary data. My screening call is: **G1 partial, G2 fail, G3 fail, G4 partial, G5 unclear, G6 pass/partial. Class B.** [Nature+2Nature+2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41578-020-00243-2?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
2564: 
2565: **5) Ordinary quantum-circuit substrates — hard reject as wedge substrate, useful only as implementation layer.**  
2566: Quantum circuits are sequences of gates, measurements, resets, and classical control; measurement is an explicit instruction that maps chosen qubits into chosen classical bits. That means locality and hidden bulk are native, but boundary semantics is not: readout is inserted by the designer through measurement instructions and classical registers. So circuits can host encodings of your structure, but do not native

769: 1. **Extract the smallest exact Prime family from source.**  
770:     Do not start from generic $m=4$ amplituhedron machinery. Start from the Prime paper’s local sign-flip spaces and isolate the first finite family the paper itself singles out: the maximal-sign-flip octagons. The output needed here is:
771:     * exact native object,
772:     * exact membership/sign-flip predicate,
773:     * exact boundary strata,
774:     * exact adjacency/incidence graph for the octagon family. [Springer](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP01%282021%29035)
775: 2. **Then extract the first gluing layer.**  
776:     The next exact object after isolated octagons is the paper’s pentagon gluing data: which combinations of sign-flip spaces assemble into the local pentagon geometries, and how those combinations land on Prime rather than the original amplituhedron. This is where the new substrate row appears: $$\texttt{boundary\_determined\_vs\_bulk\_sensitive}.$$ Anything surviving from amplituhedron to Prime is boundary-determined; anything that breaks is bulk-sensitive. [Springer](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP01%282021%29035)
777: 3. **Keep Binary/Winding in the audit lane.**  
778:     `Unwinding` says the amplituhedron has an equivalent combinatorial/topological characterization by maximal generalized winding, equivalently maximal sign flips after projection to one dimension. That makes it a **representation-equivalence theorem** for the amplituhedron side, not the next family object. Use it only after the Prime extraction to test whether the sign-flip substrate rows you currently trust are representation-free. [Springer+1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP01%282018%29016)

21232: For every family record compute
21233: 
21234: 1. Descent pattern D: ordered multiset of codim-1 limits you can reach by successively killing variables (encode as a binary string of length dim).
21235: 2. Grouped-carrier spectrum C: multiset of dimensions of connected components appearing on each descent limit.
21236: 3. Factorization matrix F: square matrix
21237:     F_ij = 1 if canonical form factorizes into product of forms when facets i and j simultaneously vanish; 0 otherwise.
21238: 4. Primitive-parameter rank vector P: ranks of Jacobian of primitive parameters evaluated on every top-face.
21239: 5. Equality-layer quiver Q: directed graph where nodes = equality layers, arrows = inclusion; store its Laplacian spectrum for comparison.
21240: 6. Residue recursion depth R: maximum number of residues that can be taken before the form freezes to a constant.
21241: 
21242: Concatenate these into a fixed-length row
21243: 
21244: row = (D | C | flatten(F) | P | spectrum(Q) | R)
21245: 
21246: All entries are integers / bits → perfect for hashing and distance metrics.
21247: 
21248: ⸻

714: **Prime is the next real family extraction.**  
715: The 2021 paper says all local positive spaces relevant for one-loop MHV are characterized by sign-flip conditions; in the maximal sign-flip case they are **finite one-loop octagons**. It then says particular combinations of these sign-flip spaces glue into local pentagon geometries that do **not** triangulate the original amplituhedron but rather its twin **Amplituhedron-Prime**, which has the **same boundary structure** and therefore the same logarithmic form, while differing in the **bulk** as a geometric space. That is a genuinely new family-level signal, not just a reformulation. [Springer+2Springer+2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP01%282021%29035)
716: 
717: That means the next exact substrate question is no longer “does sign-flip survive another presentation?” It is sharper:
718: 
719: $$\text{which substrate constraints are boundary-determined, and which are bulk-sensitive?}$$
720: 
721: Prime is the first paper in your set that directly attacks that row, because it supplies two geometries with the **same boundary/log form data** but different bulk geometry. If a candidate substrate constraint survives from amplituhedron to Prime, it is plausibly boundary-determined. If it fails, it is bulk-sensitive. None of associahedron/cosmohedron/amplituhedron-$m=1$/surfaceology currently gives you that discriminator. [Springer+1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP01%282021%29035)
722: 
723: So the next exact work should be:
724: 
725: 1. **Binary/Winding: reclassify, do not promote to new family.**  
726:     Treat it as a source-backed equivalence layer on the amplituhedron side:
727:     * exact source statement: amplituhedron $=$ maximal winding / maximal sign-flip characterization;
728:     * exact use in your program: check whether the currently extracted $m=1$ substrate rows are representation-invariant;
729:     * output: a correction in the cross-family summary, not a new family count table. [ar5iv+2ar5iv+2](https://ar5iv.org/abs/1704.05069)
730: 2. **Prime: first exact extraction target.**

145: The original six: Combinatorics of the Cosmohedron, Cosmohedra 2025, The Amplituhedron 2014, Into the Amplituhedron 2014, Scattering Forms (associahedron), Surfaceology for Colored Yukawa.
146: Plus the two additions I prioritized: Amplituhedron-Prime (Langer-Trnka-Herrmann-Zheng 2020), Binary and Winding Numbers characterization.
147: Given the goal is substrate constraints that hold robustly across positive geometry as a class, not depth in any one family:
148: The right approach is breadth at moderate depth across several families, rather than matching cosmohedron's depth everywhere.
149: Why: your substrate constraints become trustworthy through cross-family survival, not through depth in any single family. A constraint that survives five families at moderate depth is stronger evidence than a constraint confirmed at extreme depth in one family. The cosmohedron depth was necessary to have a rich enough first case to extract substantive constraints from. The other families don't need to match that depth — they need to be deep enough to test whether those constraints survive.
150: Practical sequencing:
151: First priority: surfaceology. Different construction entirely (curves on surfaces vs combinatorial nestings). If unit codimension descent survives here, it's strong evidence for substrate-level status. If it fails, you've found the boundary of where the constraint applies. This is the sharpest single test you can run.
152: Second priority: Amplituhedron-Prime. Tests the boundary-vs-bulk distinction. Same boundary as amplituhedron, different bulk. This gives you information no other family can give — whether your substrate constraints are sensitive to boundary structure only or also to bulk structure. Architecturally important for the substrate specification.
153: Third priority: Binary and Winding Numbers characterization of amplituhedron. Tests representation-independence. If the constraints you extract from geometric formulation match what you'd extract from sign-flip formulation, the constraints are representation-free. If they differ, the substrate has to preserve specific representation structure.
154: Lower priority: extending amplituhedron from m=1 to m=4. Useful but redundant with what Prime and Binary/Winding already test, and more labor-intensive. Skip unless cross-family results suggest you specifically need m=4 to resolve something.
155: Lower priority still: Scalar-Scaffolded Gluons, Loops of Loops, Wilson Loops with negative geometry. These extend scope rather than test cross-family constraints. After you have the substrate specification from the cross-family comparison, these papers tell you how far the specification extends. Not needed for the substrate extraction itself.
156: What this gives you as output:
157: A substrate specification backed by cosmohedron (deep), associahedron (deep baseline), amplituhedron m=1 (tractable cut), surfaceology (different construction), Amplituhedron-Prime (boundary-vs-bulk test), and Binary/Winding (representation test). That's six families testing six dimensions. Constraints that survive that cross-section are the core substrate specification. Constraints that fail at any point tell you where family-specific extensions are needed.
158: This is enough to produce a first substrate specification that's rigorously cross-validated. Whether that specification translates to buildable hardware is the next phase, but the specification itself would be robust.
159: Time estimate, given your rate: probably days not weeks for the three new extractions, given what you've demonstrated. Maybe a week total to have the full six-family cross-comparison running and producing integrated substrate output.
160: After that you're at the decision point about whether cross-family signal is strong enough to commit to substrate-engineering phase, or whether the signal reveals the wedge thesis needs modification.

21360: 
21361: 1. **Regex scraping equations from papers is fantasy-level brittle**  
21362:     “Scrape facet equations using ε-regular expressions → SymPy” is not serious for these papers. The definitions are not just clean equation lists. They are embedded in prose, diagrams, recursive combinatorics, conventions, and sometimes implicit constructions.
21363: 2. **Canonical form extraction is not automatic like that**  
21364:     The canonical form is often the object of the paper, not a field you scrape. Sometimes it is recursive, triangulation-dependent, defined by residues, or only indirectly characterized.
21365: 3. **“Detect facets whose equation kills exactly one variable” is too naive**  
21366:     Descent/factorization limits in positive geometry are not generally “kill one variable.” They can be boundary strata, degeneration limits, nested structures, tube contractions, residues, soft limits, or equality-saturation regimes.
